title: Tortoiseshell-stitch Japanese binding, the kikko pattern
subtitle: The hexagon-scaled Japanese stab binding pattern
excerpt: Kikko means tortoiseshell in Japanese. The binding pattern produces a repeating hexagonal scale across the spine, a more intricate variant of the four-hole yotsume stitch that uses the same basic stab-sewing method on thicker boards.
sourceNotes: The kikko (tortoiseshell) stab-binding pattern is one of several traditional Japanese stab-sewing patterns documented in bookbinding literature on the Japanese binding tradition. Primary source is the oral and demonstrated tradition preserved in Japanese craft texts and brought to Western bookbinding via the Arts and Crafts movement. No single pre-1928 printed source is definitively authoritative on the full kikko pattern; this tutorial synthesises documented stitch paths consistent with published diagrams of the binding type.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding where the needle passes through the full thickness of the text block from front to back, rather than through a fold. The pages cannot be spread fully flat.
The Japanese word for tortoiseshell; also the name of the bookbinding stitch pattern that produces a hexagonal scale motif on the spine.

content.text: method as the four-hole yotsume stitch. The sewing path wraps each hole in two directions to build the hexagonal motif. Thread shows on both front and back boards, so the pattern looks the same on both sides.

attrs.title: Cutting tool safety
attrs.body: The AWL passes through the full depth of the text block. Work on a firm, stable surface. Hold the text block securely from the side while piercing to prevent it shifting. Change the awl tip if it bends, a bent tip wanders off the pierced line.

items.name: Text block
items.notes: Stab binding works on unfolded single sheets. Pages cannot open fully flat, so limit the text block to 80 sheets (160 pages) at most. Heavier text blocks split the hole edges.

items.name: Bookbinding board, 1.5 mm
items.notes: Front and back covers, cut to the same height as the text block and 5 mm wider at the foredge, flush at the spine.

items.name: Bookbinding thread, linen (waxed)
items.notes: A contrasting or complementary colour shows the kikko pattern well. 4-cord waxed linen.

items.name: Bookbinding awl
items.notes: For piercing sewing stations through the full thickness.

content.text: The kikko pattern uses a double row of holes. Rule a line 5 mm from the spine edge. Rule a second line 10 mm from the spine edge. On the first line, mark holes at 15 mm, then every 20 mm. On the second line, mark holes at 25 mm (offset by one half-step), then every 20 mm. This offset produces the hexagonal rhythm when the sewing path crosses between the two rows.

Clamp the text block between its covers. Pierce each marked hole straight through with the awl, perpendicular to the spine. Work through all layers at once, misaligned holes produce a lopsided pattern. A wooden press or two G-clamps keep the block stable during piercing.

The sewing path is too complex to follow from text alone; use a printed diagram. The needle passes front to back at each hole and wraps the thread around the spine edge between certain holes to form the hexagon sides. One full pass completes the pattern; a second pass in the same direction doubles the coverage.

items.cause: The hole spacing or alignment was inconsistent.
items.fix: Mark holes with a template rather than measuring each one individually. A strip of card with the hole positions punched is a reliable template for repeating the pattern across multiple books.

items.symptom: Thread cuts the edge of the paper at the spine
items.cause: Holes are too close to the spine edge, or the thread is too heavy.
items.fix: Move the first row of holes to at least 7 mm from the spine edge, and use finer thread for thin or fragile paper.
